title: detection and Management of Abuse of Androgenic performanceEnhancing Drugs

items.title: Why androgenic PEDs dominate performance-enhancing drug abuse
items.text: Androgens at pharmacologic doses are the only drug class with definitive evidence of enhancing strength and athletic performance; they are the most commonly abused PEDs in clinical practice.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "Androgens administered at pharmacological dosages are the most commonly used PEDs, and they are the only class of drugs with definitive evidence that they enhance strength and athletic performance."

items.title: How androgenic PED abusers typically present
items.text: Chronic abusers present with infertility or requests for testosterone therapy; semen analyses show azoospermia or very low sperm counts with suppressed gonadotropins.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "The common clinical presentations of androgenic PED abuse are male infertility and requests for testosterone prescriptions to treat low serum testosterone."

items.title: How serum hormones differ by androgenic PED type
items.text: Exogenous testosterone, precursors, and hCG raise testosterone and suppress gonadotropins; nontestosterone anabolic steroids lower testosterone and gonadotropins; aromatase inhibitors and clomiphene raise testosterone and gonadotropins.
items.reference: Diagnosis of Androgenic PED Abuse: "The use of nontestosterone androgenic anabolic steroids (eg, nadrolone, oxandrolone, danazol, stanozolol, or tetrahydrogestrinone) is associated with low testosterone and gonadotropin concentrations."

items.title: Why muscular physique with very low testosterone suggests PED abuse
items.text: Discordance between muscularity and suppressed gonadotropins/low testosterone, elevated hematocrit, and low SHBG/HDL support abuse; re-ask respectfully about use before expensive pituitary workup.
items.reference: Case 1: "This man has a discordance between his physical appearance (muscularity) and his serum testosterone concentration (very low), and he is a member of a social group at higher risk for use of androgenic PEDs (weightlifter at a gym)."

items.title: How to support clinical suspicion of androgenic PED abuse
items.text: Measure hematocrit plus HDL cholesterol, SHBG, and/or thyroxine-binding globulin—androgens suppress HDL, SHBG, and TBG; cortisol-binding globulin is unaffected.
items.reference: Diagnosis of Androgenic PED Abuse: "Androgens raise serum hematocrit and decrease serum HDL cholesterol, SHBG, and thyroxine-binding globulin."

items.title: Why urine anti-doping panels are not used clinically
items.text: WADA methods require observed random sampling; outpatients can dilute, substitute, or time samples—commercial labs lack widespread access.
items.reference: Diagnosis of Androgenic PED Abuse: "methods to accurately measure androgenic PEDs and their metabolites in urine are not relevant diagnostic studies in clinical practice."

items.title: How to approach patients who abuse androgenic PEDs
items.text: Establish trust through respectful nonjudgmental counseling; screen for anxiety, depression, and substance use; query readiness to cease before selecting therapy.
items.reference: Treatment of Androgenic PED Abuse and Withdrawal Syndrome: "A key principle for the care of androgenic PED users is the establishment a relationship of trust based on respectful and nonjudgmental behavior."

items.title: Why men with ≤1 year PED use need no adjunctive hormones to quit
items.text: Gonadotropins normalize within 3–6 months and testosterone within 6 months in most short-term users; spermatogenesis recovers 3–6 months after testosterone normalizes.
items.reference: Treatment of Androgenic PED Abuse and Withdrawal Syndrome: "Immediate discontinuation of androgenic PEDs without additional medical therapy is the best choice for men who have used these drugs for 1 year or less."

items.title: How to restore fertility after chronic androgenic PED abuse
items.text: For men using PEDs >1 year who want conception within 2 years, hCG or clomiphene may hasten recovery; exogenous testosterone continues to suppress spermatogenesis.
items.reference: Treatment of Androgenic PED Abuse and Withdrawal Syndrome: "For men who are infertile because of androgenic PED use for more than 1 year and who want to have a child within the next 2 years, the best options may be hCG therapy or clomiphene therapy."

items.title: Why androgenic PED withdrawal causes mood symptoms
items.text: An androgenic PED withdrawal syndrome of depressed mood, anxiety, and low self-esteem may follow acute cessation, especially after chronic high-dose stacking.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "there is increasing evidence that there is an androgenic PED withdrawal syndrome of depressed mood, anxiety, and low self-esteem after acute cessation."

items.title: How 'conversion' to prescription testosterone may help reluctant quitters
items.text: IM testosterone at 2–3× replacement dose with gradual taper can be safer than unregulated high-dose stacking and builds clinician trust over time.
items.reference: Treatment of Androgenic PED Abuse and Withdrawal Syndrome: "Conversion to intramuscular testosterone formulations at up to 2 to 3 times the usual replacement dosage with a tapering of the dosage over several months is likely to be safer than very high dosages of androgenic PEDs of unknown safety and purity purchased from unregulated sources."

items.title: Documented health consequences of androgenic PED abuse in men
items.text: Proven consequences include infertility, acne, and erythrocytosis; oral alkylated androgens cause hepatopathy; tendon ruptures and cardiovascular/psychiatric risks remain concerning though confounded.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "The proven health consequences include infertility, acne, and erythrocytosis in men."

items.question: A muscular weightlifter has undetectable LH/FSH, low testosterone, and hematocrit 46%. Best additional testing?
options: Urinary steroid metabolite mass spectrometry panel
items.explanation: Low SHBG and HDL support androgen exposure; urine anti-doping panels are not clinically available and are easily gamed.
items.reference: Case 1: "Answer: B) Serum lipid panel and SHBG measurement"

items.question: A man used stacked PEDs for 1 year and wants to stop. Best recommendation besides cessation?
items.explanation: Short-term users recover spontaneously within 6–12 months; exogenous testosterone or hCG may delay axis recovery.
items.reference: Case 1 (Continued): "Answer: A) No additional therapy"

items.question: After years of PED abuse, 2 months off, testosterone 80 ng/dL, azoospermia, couple trying to conceive. Best therapy?
items.explanation: Chronic abuse with persistent hypogonadism and infertility warrants hCG to restore testosterone and spermatogenesis; testosterone would suppress fertility further.
items.reference: Case 1 (Continued): "Answer: C) hCG therapy"

items.question: A 55-year-old runner has new gynecomastia, testosterone 770 ng/dL, LH 13, FSH 10. Most likely cause?
items.explanation: High testosterone with elevated gonadotropins suggests SERMs like clomiphene; hCG and androstenedione would suppress gonadotropins.
items.reference: Case 3: "Answer: B) Clomiphene abuse"

items.question: Nontestosterone anabolic androgenic steroid use typically shows:
items.explanation: Exogenous non-testosterone androgens suppress the axis and lower measured testosterone.
items.reference: Table. Serum Hormone Concentrations During Androgenic PED Use: "Nontestosterone anabolic androgenic steroid"

items.question: During hCG abuse as a PED, expected hormones are:
options: High testosterone, low LH and FSH
Low testosterone, high LH and FSH
items.explanation: hCG mimics LH, raising testosterone while suppressing pituitary gonadotropins.
items.reference: Table. Serum Hormone Concentrations During Androgenic PED Use: "hCG"

items.question: Lifetime prevalence of ever using androgenic PEDs in men worldwide is approximately:
items.explanation: Lifetime prevalence is probably 1%–5% though chronic abuse is lower.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "the lifetime prevalence of ever using these PEDs is probably 1% to 5% in men worldwide."

items.question: Semen analysis in chronic androgenic PED abusers typically shows:
options: Azoospermia or very low sperm concentration
Only decreased motility with normal count
items.explanation: Suppressed intratesticular testosterone shuts down spermatogenesis.
items.reference: Diagnosis of Androgenic PED Abuse: "Seminal fluid analyses generally show azoospermia or very low sperm concentrations."

items.question: After discontinuing short-term PED use, gonadotropins typically normalize within:
items.explanation: Most men with ≤1 year use recover axis function within months.
items.reference: Treatment of Androgenic PED Abuse and Withdrawal Syndrome: "serum gonadotropin concentrations spontaneously normalize within 3 to 6 months"

items.question: Which finding is sensitive and specific for PED abuse?
options: No single physical finding is sensitive and specific
items.explanation: Gynecomastia and small testes may be present but are not sensitive or specific; testicular size may remain normal if baseline volume was large.
items.reference: Diagnosis of Androgenic PED Abuse: "gynecomastia and small testes, but these findings are not sensitive or specific."

items.question: Hepatopathy with androgenic PEDs occurs with:
items.explanation: Oral 17-alkylated androgens carry hepatotoxicity risk.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "Hepatopathy occurs with use of oral alkylated androgens only"

items.question: For a patient unwilling to stop PEDs, a harm-reduction option includes:
options: Conversion to prescription testosterone with gradual taper
items.explanation: Conversion may be safer than unregulated products and builds therapeutic alliance.
items.reference: Treatment of Androgenic PED Abuse and Withdrawal Syndrome: "Some men who are not willing to discontinue androgenic PEDs are willing to "convert" to prescription testosterone."

items.question: Definitive diagnosis of PED abuse in the general public requires:
options: Patient disclosure—no definitive test except in elite sport settings
items.explanation: Except for disclosure, no definitive outpatient diagnostic method exists.
items.reference: Barriers to Optimal Practice: "Except for disclosure of androgenic abuse by the patient, there are no methods of making a definitive diagnosis of androgenic PED abuse in members of the general public."

items.statement: Eugonadal men taking pharmacologic androgens for performance are abusing PEDs.
items.explanation: Replacement in hypogonadal patients is not abuse; use in eugonadal men is.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "Individuals who are eugonadal and take androgenic PEDs are abusing them; prescription of androgen and gonadotropin replacement to hypogonadal patients is not PED abuse."

items.statement: Gonadal axis function can recover spontaneously after discontinuation of androgenic PEDs.
items.explanation: Recovery timeline depends on duration and intensity of abuse.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "Recovery of gonadal axis function occurs spontaneously after discontinuation of androgenic PEDs."

items.statement: Chronic female androgenic PED abusers are commonly seen in general endocrinology practice.
items.explanation: Long-term abusers are almost exclusively male elite athletes—rare in most practices.
items.reference: Clinical Presentation of Androgenic PED Abuse: "Women who are chronic androgenic abusers are almost exclusively elite athletes and are rarely seen by most endocrinologists"

items.explanation: Low SHBG supports clinical suspicion alongside low HDL.
items.reference: Diagnosis of Androgenic PED Abuse: "Androgens raise serum hematocrit and decrease serum HDL cholesterol, SHBG, and thyroxine-binding globulin."

items.statement: Intramuscular testosterone taper preserves fertility during recovery from PED abuse.
items.explanation: Exogenous testosterone continues to suppress spermatogenesis.
items.reference: Treatment of Androgenic PED Abuse and Withdrawal Syndrome: "Exogenous testosterone therapy will continue to suppress spermatogenesis and fertility."

items.statement: Clomiphene abuse can raise testosterone and gonadotropins.
items.explanation: SERMs block estrogen negative feedback, increasing LH/FSH and testosterone.
items.reference: Table. Serum Hormone Concentrations During Androgenic PED Use: "Clomiphene"

items.statement: Anabolic-androgenic steroid users have increased risk of tendon rupture.
items.explanation: Upper-extremity tendon ruptures are reported especially in steroid users.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "there appears to be increased risk of tendinous ruptures, particularly in muscles of the arms and chest."

items.statement: Social stigma is a barrier to disclosure of androgenic PED abuse.
items.explanation: Stigma and anti-doping laws limit honest reporting.
items.reference: Barriers to Optimal Practice: "Social stigma and laws against androgenic PED abuse are barriers to disclosure of abuse."

items.assertion: Assertion: Chronic androgenic PED abusers often present with infertility.
items.reason: Reason: Androgens stimulate spermatogenesis at pharmacologic doses.
items.explanation: Assertion true; exogenous androgens suppress the HPG axis and impair sperm production—reason false.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "Serum gonadotropins are suppressed, and seminal fluid analyses show azoospermia or very low sperm concentrations."

items.assertion: Assertion: Men with ≤1 year PED use should stop without adjunctive hormone therapy.
items.reason: Reason: All men require hCG after any PED exposure.
items.explanation: Assertion true for short-term users—reason false.
items.reference: Treatment of Androgenic PED Abuse and Withdrawal Syndrome: "Immediate discontinuation of androgenic PEDs without additional medical therapy is the best choice for men who have used these drugs for 1 year or less."

items.assertion: Assertion: hCG therapy may restore spermatogenesis in chronic PED abusers seeking conception.
items.reason: Reason: hCG has no LH activity on Leydig cells.
items.explanation: Assertion true (Case 1 continued)—reason false; hCG mimics LH.
items.reference: Case 1 (Continued): "Treatment with hCG therapy (Answer C) (that has LH) is likely to be effective in restoring his normal endogenous testosterone concentrations and may restore spermatogenesis within 6 to 12 months."

items.assertion: Assertion: Clomiphene abuse can cause gynecomastia with high-normal testosterone and elevated gonadotropins.
items.explanation: Assertion true (Case 3)—clomiphene raises gonadotropins; reason false.
items.reference: Case 3: "high-normal serum testosterone and serum gonadotropins suggests the use of a selective estrogen receptor modulator such as clomiphene"

items.assertion: Assertion: Urine steroid panels are not useful for routine clinical diagnosis of PED abuse.
items.reason: Reason: Elite sport testing methods are easily applied to unobserved outpatient samples.
items.explanation: Assertion true; outpatient samples are easily falsified—reason false.
items.reference: Diagnosis of Androgenic PED Abuse: "methods to accurately measure androgenic PEDs and their metabolites in urine are not relevant diagnostic studies in clinical practice."

items.assertion: Assertion: Respectful nonjudgmental care improves engagement of PED users.
items.reason: Reason: PED users universally trust scientists about adverse effects without question.
items.explanation: Assertion true; users may distrust clinicians who denied benefits for decades—reason false.
items.reference: Treatment of Androgenic PED Abuse and Withdrawal Syndrome: "They are often distrustful of traditional health care providers and scientists."

items.assertion: Assertion: Erythrocytosis is a proven adverse effect of androgenic PED abuse in men.
items.explanation: Assertion true—reason false; androgens raise hematocrit.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "The proven health consequences include infertility, acne, and erythrocytosis in men."

items.assertion: Assertion: Anastrozole has weaker evidence than gonadotropin therapy for PED-related infertility.
items.reason: Reason: Aromatase inhibitors are first-line with robust RCT data in this setting.
items.explanation: Assertion true per Case 1—anastrozole is anecdotal; hCG has stronger evidence.
items.reference: Case 1 (Continued): "there is much stronger evidence for effectiveness with gonadotropin replacement therapy."
